structured_choices.data_source: Histopathological image dataset of approximately 5,000 breast cancer patients collected over 10 years from hospital records/biobank archives. Each sample includes: (1) digitized histopathological slide image, (2) clinical risk score (demographic and medical history features), (3) patient survival outcome (time-to-event or binary survival status at fixed follow-up horizon). Data sourced from a single institution or consortium with consistent image acquisition protocols where possible; if multi-source, acquisition metadata is recorded.
structured_choices.label_definition: Primary outcome: overall survival measured as time-to-event (days from diagnosis to death or censoring date) or binary survival status at a fixed follow-up horizon (e.g., 5-year survival). The choice between time-to-event and binary classification should match the oncologist's decision-making context. For this design, assume continuous time-to-event regression with right censoring handled via appropriate loss functions (e.g., concordance index or accelerated failure time models). The label is extracted from institutional medical records with date verification and missingness documented.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified temporal + site split (if multi-center) or stratified random split (if single-center). Rationale: Temporal stratification is avoided here because survival outcomes have inherent follow-up delays; instead, stratify on: (1) survival quartiles (to preserve outcome distribution), (2) patient age groups, (3) tumor stage/grade if available, and (4) data acquisition site (if multi-center). Use 60% train / 20% validation / 20% test. Temporal sequencing is not appropriate because the hypothesis concerns image analysis capability, not temporal drift. Stratification ensures all outcome ranges and demographic subgroups are represented in each split, reducing confounding from imbalanced allocation.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: Image preprocessing pipeline (stain normalization, rotation correction, artifact removal) is fit on the training set only, using reference images or aggregated statistics (e.g., mean color matrix for stain normalization) computed from train images. This fitted preprocessing is applied deterministically to validation and test images. Clinical risk score features (if scaling or encoding needed) are fit on the train set. All transformers, scaling parameters, and normalization statistics are locked after fitting and applied without re-fitting to validation or test.
structured_choices.features: Multimodal input: (1) CNN features extracted from histopathological images (raw pixel input to CNN or pre-computed embeddings from a pre-trained backbone, justified below), (2) clinical risk score features (demographic variables, medical history). The CNN processes images end-to-end; clinical features are concatenated to the final CNN layer representation before the regression head. Alternatively, clinical features can be used as a separate baseline pathway for comparison. No hand-crafted image features (SIFT, texture descriptors) are engineered; the CNN learns representations. Clinical features should not include proxy variables for survival (e.g., treatment received post-diagnosis if treatment was chosen based on prognosis). Raw pixel values are normalized to [0, 1] or z-scored per image channel.
structured_choices.model: CNN-based regression model: ResNet-50 or similar pre-trained backbone (ImageNet weights) with final fully-connected layers replaced by a regression head predicting continuous survival time (or quantile regression for prediction intervals). Input: histopathological images (e.g., 512×512 or 256×256 patches) + concatenated clinical features. Output: predicted survival time (in days) or survival probability. The model is trained end-to-end with joint optimization of image and clinical signal, or clinical features are appended only at the final layer to preserve image representation learning.
structured_choices.baseline: Clinical risk score model: A linear regression (or generalized linear model / accelerated failure time model) trained on clinical features alone. The baseline receives identical tuning effort: hyperparameters (L2 regularization, learning rate if using gradient boosting) are tuned on the validation set using the same primary metric (concordance index or MAE). The baseline uses the same train/val/test split and identical label definitions. This ensures the comparison isolates the value of image analysis. A secondary baseline (CNN on images alone, without clinical features) may also be fit to determine whether images alone outperform clinical scores.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Concordance Index (C-index) for time-to-event prediction. Justification: C-index measures the probability that, for a random pair of patients, the model correctly ranks the patient with earlier death as having lower predicted survival time. It is the standard metric in survival analysis, handles censored data correctly, and directly reflects clinical utility—oncologists care about ranking patients by risk to prioritize interventions. C-index ranges [0, 1] with 0.5 representing random ranking and 1.0 perfect ranking. Statistical significance is tested via bootstrap confidence intervals on the test set.
secondary_metrics: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) in survival time prediction (in months or years), as specified in success_metric_context
Integrated Brier Score (IBS) to assess calibration and discrimination simultaneously
Time-dependent AUC at fixed follow-up horizons (e.g., 1-year, 3-year, 5-year survival) to assess performance at clinically relevant timepoints
Calibration plots and Hosmer–Lemeshow test to verify predicted survival probabilities match observed frequencies
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (20% of data, held-out during training). Hyperparameters for both CNN and baseline are tuned by grid search or random search on the validation set, with early stopping to prevent overfitting. The metric used for selection is the primary metric (C-index). Once hyperparameters are finalized, the test set is used only once at the end for final reporting.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is held out throughout model development and hyperparameter tuning. After model selection is complete on the validation set, the final model (with locked hyperparameters and weights trained on train+validation combined, or trained on train only with validation used for early stopping) is evaluated on the test set exactly once. Test set results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals. No data leakage or iterative tuning on test data is permitted.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confound: patient demographics and treatment history. Controls: (1) stratify train/val/test by age group and tumor stage to ensure balanced representation, (2) compute C-index separately for demographic subgroups (e.g., age <50 vs. ≥50, stage I vs. II vs. III+) to detect differential performance and hidden bias, (3) if treatment assignment differs between image-analyzed vs. clinically-scored cohorts, include treatment as a covariate in the baseline model to isolate image-specific signal. The experiment tests whether CNN-derived image features improve prediction beyond what clinical scores capture, not whether demographics alone explain outcomes. A sensitivity analysis repeats the analysis after stratifying or matching patients on age and stage to demonstrate robustness. Multi-center data (if applicable) is handled via site-stratified splits and site-specific performance reporting.

design_narrative:
To test whether convolutional neural networks analyzing histopathological images predict breast cancer survival better than clinical risk scores, the following experiment is proposed:

**1. Data and Labeling**
The experiment uses the available dataset of approximately 5,000 patient samples collected over 10 years, each with a digitized histopathological image, clinical risk score (demographic and medical history variables), and survival outcome. The primary label is overall survival, defined as time-to-event (days from diagnosis to death or censoring) with appropriate handling of right-censored patients. Outcome labels are extracted from institutional medical records with date verification. Any missing images or outcome data points are excluded; missingness is documented and reported.

**2. Data Split Strategy**
The data is divided into training (60%), validation (20%), and test (20%) sets using stratified random sampling. Stratification occurs on: (i) survival time quartiles to preserve outcome distribution, (ii) patient age groups, (iii) tumor stage/grade, and (iv) acquisition site if multi-center data. Stratified splitting is chosen because it prevents outcome imbalance and confounding by demographic variables; temporal splits are avoided because the hypothesis concerns image analysis capability, not temporal generalization. Leakage is prevented by fitting all preprocessing pipelines (image stain normalization, feature scaling) on the training set only and applying them deterministically to validation and test sets.

**3. Image Preprocessing and Feature Engineering**
Histopathological images undergo standardization to mitigate staining and acquisition variability: (a) stain normalization using Macenko or similar method, fit to the training set's reference images, (b) rotation correction if present, (c) artifact removal (e.g., tissue folds, pen marks) via automated detection or manual flagging. Normalized images are then passed to the CNN without hand-crafted features; the network learns representations end-to-end. Clinical features (demographics, medical history) are concatenated to the CNN's final representation layer before the regression output head, enabling multimodal fusion. All preprocessing parameters and scaling statistics are locked after training and applied without re-fitting to held-out data.

**4. Model Architecture and Baseline**
The primary model is a CNN-based regressor: a ResNet-50 backbone (initialized with ImageNet weights) with a regression head predicting continuous survival time. Clinical features are fused at the final layer. The baseline is a linear/accelerated failure time regression model trained on clinical features alone. Critically, the baseline receives identical tuning effort: hyperparameters (regularization, learning rate) are optimized on the validation set using the same primary metric (concordance index), ensuring fair comparison. A secondary baseline (CNN on images alone) is also fit to isolate the contribution of image analysis.

**5. Evaluation Metrics**
The primary metric is the Concordance Index (C-index), the standard for survival analysis. It measures the probability that the model correctly ranks patient pairs by risk and naturally handles censored data. C-index directly reflects clinical utility: oncologists use risk rankings to prioritize interventions. Secondary metrics include Mean Absolute Error (MAE) in survival time, Integrated Brier Score (calibration), and time-dependent AUC at fixed horizons (1-year, 3-year, 5-year survival). Calibration plots verify that predicted survival probabilities match observed frequencies.

**6. Model Selection and Validation**
Hyperparameters for both CNN and baseline are tuned on the validation set via grid or random search, optimizing the C-index. Early stopping is applied during training. The test set is touched only once, after hyperparameter selection is complete. Test performance is reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals; statistical significance is assessed via bootstrap resampling.

**7. Confound Controls and Scope**
To address confounding by patient demographics and treatment history: (i) stratify train/val/test by age and stage, (ii) compute C-index separately for demographic subgroups to detect bias, (iii) include treatment as a covariate in baseline models if treatment assignment varies, (iv) conduct sensitivity analyses with matched or stratified cohorts. The experiment tests whether image features improve prediction beyond clinical scores; it does not establish causation or clinical utility in isolation—deployment requires prospective validation and stakeholder approval.
